summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the definition of 'Two-sample t-test'?

"Two-sample t-test" means: Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂). The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Two-sample t-interval'?

"Two-sample t-interval" means: (x̄₁ - x̄₂) ± t*·√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂); estimates the true difference in population means. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Welch's degrees of freedom'?

"Welch's degrees of freedom" means: A formula approximating df for unequal variances; generally calculated by technology rather than by hand. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Conservative df'?

"Conservative df" means: Using df = min(n₁-1, n₂-1) when technology is unavailable; produces wider intervals and larger p-values. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Conditions for two-sample t'?

"Conditions for two-sample t" means: Random and independent samples, both n < 10% of respective populations, and both populations approximately normal or both samples large (n ≥ 30). The other options describe different concepts.
